subject: Economics
chapter: Market Equilibrium (Equilibrium Price, Excess Demand/Supply, Price Ceiling and Price Floor)

questions.question: Market equilibrium is defined as a situation where:
options: Quantity demanded equals quantity supplied at a given price
Price is set by the government
questions.explanation: Market equilibrium occurs at the price where Qd = Qs. At this price, there is no tendency for price to change, and the market clears completely with no surplus or shortage.

questions.question: The price at which market equilibrium is achieved is called:
questions.explanation: The equilibrium price (also called market-clearing price) is the price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, leaving no excess demand or excess supply in the market.

questions.question: Excess demand in a market refers to a situation where:
options: Market price is above equilibrium price
Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the prevailing price
Both demand and supply are equal
questions.explanation: Excess demand (shortage) occurs when Qd > Qs at the prevailing price. This situation arises when the market price is below the equilibrium price, causing buyers to demand more than sellers supply.

questions.question: Excess supply in a market is defined as:
options: A situation where Qs > Qd at the prevailing market price
A situation where Qd > Qs at the prevailing market price
A situation where the government fixes price above equilibrium
A situation where the market is in equilibrium
questions.explanation: Excess supply (surplus) occurs when Qs > Qd. This typically arises when the prevailing market price is above the equilibrium price, causing sellers to supply more than buyers are willing to purchase.

questions.question: A price ceiling is best defined as:
options: A minimum price set by the government above which price cannot fall
A maximum price set by the government below which the actual price cannot fall
A maximum price set by the government above which the actual price cannot rise
A price set equal to the equilibrium price by the government
questions.explanation: A price ceiling is a legal maximum price imposed by the government. The market price cannot legally exceed this ceiling. It is effective only when set below the equilibrium price, creating shortage/excess demand.

questions.question: A price floor is defined as:
options: A minimum price below which the market price cannot legally fall
A maximum price above which the market price cannot legally rise
The price at which the market clears
A price at which excess supply is zero
questions.explanation: A price floor is a legal minimum price set by the government. The market price cannot legally go below the floor. It is effective only when set above the equilibrium price, creating surplus/excess supply.

questions.question: Which of the following is a real-world example of a price floor in India?
options: Maximum Retail Price (MRP) on packaged goods
Minimum Support Price (MSP) for agricultural commodities
questions.explanation: Minimum Support Price (MSP) is a classic example of a price floor — the government sets a minimum price for agricultural goods (like wheat, rice) to protect farmers' income. It is set above equilibrium to ensure farmers receive adequate returns.

questions.question: Which of the following is a real-world example of a price ceiling?
options: Rent control laws imposed by urban local bodies
questions.explanation: Rent control is a classic price ceiling — the government sets a maximum rent that landlords can charge. This keeps rent affordable for tenants but is set below the market equilibrium rent, leading to housing shortages.

questions.question: When there is excess demand in a market, what tends to happen to price?
options: Price rises until Qd equals Qs
Government intervenes to lower the price
questions.explanation: Excess demand (Qd > Qs) means buyers are competing for limited goods. This competition bids up the price. As price rises, Qd falls and Qs rises, until equilibrium is restored where Qd = Qs.

questions.question: When there is excess supply in a market, what tends to happen to price?
options: Price falls until Qs equals Qd
Government buys the surplus at market price
questions.explanation: Excess supply (Qs > Qd) means sellers have unsold inventory. To clear stock, sellers lower prices. As price falls, Qd rises and Qs falls until equilibrium is restored.

questions.question: Given: Qd = 100 – 2P and Qs = 20 + 2P. What is the equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, Qd = Qs → 100 – 2P = 20 + 2P → 80 = 4P → P = 20. Equilibrium quantity = 100 – 2(20) = 60 units. The market clears at ₹20.

questions.question: Given: Qd = 100 – 2P and Qs = 20 + 2P (Equilibrium P = 20). What is the equilibrium quantity?
questions.explanation: At P = 20: Qd = 100 – 2(20) = 60; Qs = 20 + 2(20) = 60. Both are equal at 60 units, confirming equilibrium quantity is 60 units.

questions.question: At equilibrium price in a free market, which of the following is always true?
options: There is excess demand equal to zero
There is excess supply equal to zero
Both excess demand and excess supply are zero
Price is at its maximum possible level
questions.explanation: At equilibrium price, Qd = Qs. This means neither excess demand (Qd – Qs = 0) nor excess supply (Qs – Qd = 0) exists. The market clears completely.

questions.question: A price ceiling is effective (binding) only when it is set:
options: Either above or below the equilibrium price
questions.explanation: A price ceiling is binding (effective) only when it is set below the equilibrium price. In this case, the legal maximum is lower than what the market would naturally charge, creating excess demand (shortage).

questions.question: A price floor is effective (binding) only when it is set:
questions.explanation: A price floor is binding only when set above the equilibrium price. If set below equilibrium, the market price naturally stays above the floor, making it non-binding. A floor above equilibrium creates excess supply (surplus).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the immediate effect of an effective (binding) price ceiling?
options: Surplus of goods in the market
Shortage of goods in the market
questions.explanation: An effective price ceiling (set below equilibrium) keeps price artificially low. At this low price, Qd > Qs, creating a shortage. Consumers want more than producers are willing to supply at the ceiling price.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the immediate effect of an effective (binding) price floor?
options: Shortage of goods in the market
Surplus of goods in the market
questions.explanation: An effective price floor (set above equilibrium) keeps price artificially high. At this high price, Qs > Qd, creating a surplus. Producers supply more than consumers are willing to buy at the floor price.

questions.question: The equilibrium price in a competitive market is also called:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium price is also called the market-clearing price because at this price, all goods supplied are purchased and all demand is met — the market 'clears' with no surplus or shortage remaining.

questions.question: If market price is below the equilibrium price, we observe:
options: Excess supply — producers supply more than demanded
Excess demand — consumers demand more than supplied
questions.explanation: When market price < equilibrium price: Qd > Qs (law of demand: lower price → higher Qd; law of supply: lower price → lower Qs). This creates excess demand (shortage).

questions.question: If market price is above the equilibrium price, we observe:
options: Excess demand — consumers want more than supplied
Excess supply — producers supply more than demanded
questions.explanation: When market price > equilibrium price: Qs > Qd (higher price → lower Qd; higher price → higher Qs). This creates excess supply (surplus), causing downward pressure on prices.

questions.question: Qd = 200 – 5P and Qs = 50 + 10P. At P = 8, what is the excess demand?
questions.explanation: At P = 8: Qd = 200 – 5(8) = 200 – 40 = 160; Qs = 50 + 10(8) = 50 + 80 = 130. Excess Demand = Qd – Qs = 160 – 130 = 30 units. Price is below equilibrium (P* = 10), confirming shortage.

questions.question: Qd = 200 – 5P and Qs = 50 + 10P. What is the equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Setting Qd = Qs: 200 – 5P = 50 + 10P → 150 = 15P → P = 10. At P = 10: Qd = 200 – 50 = 150; Qs = 50 + 100 = 150. Equilibrium price = ₹10, equilibrium quantity = 150 units.

questions.question: Minimum wage legislation is an example of which type of price control?
options: Price ceiling — to protect consumers from high wages
Price floor — to protect workers from earning too little
Equilibrium price — set by demand and supply of labor
Administered price — changed every year by RBI
questions.explanation: Minimum wage is a classic price floor in the labor market. It sets a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage to protect low-income workers. However, it can create excess supply of labor (unemployment) if set too high.

questions.question: When excess demand exists in a market, competitive bidding among buyers will:
options: Keep price constant while increasing supply
Reduce both demand and supply simultaneously
questions.explanation: In the presence of excess demand, buyers compete against each other to obtain the scarce goods, bidding up the price. This price rise reduces Qd and increases Qs until equilibrium is reached.

questions.question: Price ceiling is imposed by the government primarily to:
options: Protect producers from earning excess profits
Protect consumers by keeping prices affordable
Encourage greater production of essential goods
Create surplus stock for government procurement
questions.explanation: Governments impose price ceilings primarily to make essential goods (food, medicine, housing) affordable for consumers, especially low-income groups. However, this creates shortages as supply falls short of demand at the low ceiling price.

questions.question: Price floor is imposed by the government primarily to:
options: Protect consumers from paying high prices
Reduce surplus production in the economy
Protect producers by ensuring minimum returns
Eliminate excess demand in the market
questions.explanation: Governments impose price floors (like MSP) primarily to protect producers — especially farmers — from receiving below-subsistence prices due to market forces. This ensures minimum income to producers.

questions.question: Given Qd = 80 – 2P and Qs = 20 + P. What is the equilibrium price and quantity?
questions.explanation: Set Qd = Qs: 80 – 2P = 20 + P → 60 = 3P → P = 20. Equilibrium quantity: Qd = 80 – 40 = 40; Qs = 20 + 20 = 40. Equilibrium P = ₹20, Q = 40 units.

questions.question: If a price ceiling is set at the equilibrium price, the market outcome is:
options: Excess demand equal to quantity demanded
The same as the free-market outcome — no distortion
Shortage equal to the ceiling price
questions.explanation: A price ceiling set exactly at equilibrium price is non-binding. At that price, Qd = Qs already, so there is no shortage and no distortion. The ceiling has no effect on market outcome.

questions.question: Market demand schedule: at P=10, Qd=80; at P=15, Qd=60. Market supply: at P=10, Qs=40; at P=15, Qs=60. The equilibrium price is:
questions.explanation: At P=10: Qd=80 > Qs=40 → excess demand of 40. At P=15: Qd=60 = Qs=60 → equilibrium. The market clears at P=15 with Q=60 units.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about market equilibrium is INCORRECT?
options: At equilibrium, there is no tendency for price to change
Equilibrium can be disturbed by shifts in demand or supply
At equilibrium, all consumers' wants are satisfied
Equilibrium price equates quantity demanded with quantity supplied
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, Qd = Qs, meaning quantity demanded at that price equals quantity supplied. However, this does NOT mean all consumer wants are satisfied — many consumers may want more at that price but cannot afford it (constrained by income/willingness to pay).

questions.question: A black market is most likely to emerge as a consequence of:
options: A price floor set above equilibrium
An equilibrium price set by market forces
A price ceiling set below equilibrium
A surplus created by excess supply
questions.explanation: When a price ceiling is set below equilibrium, it creates a shortage — Qd > Qs. Some consumers willing to pay more than the ceiling price will transact with sellers who illegally charge above the ceiling, creating a black market.

questions.question: Government procurement (purchase of surplus goods) is typically associated with:
questions.explanation: Under a price floor (e.g., MSP), quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, creating a surplus. Since the market cannot clear this surplus at the floor price, the government often procures (purchases) the surplus to support the price floor.

questions.question: Qd = 300 – 4P and Qs = 100 + 6P. What is the equilibrium price?
Using Qd = 300 – 4P and Qs = 100 + 6P (Equilibrium P = 20), what is the excess supply at P = 25?
questions.explanation: At P=25: Qd = 300–4(25) = 300–100 = 200; Qs = 100+6(25) = 100+150 = 250. Excess Supply = Qs – Qd = 250 – 200 = 50 units. Since P=25 > P*=20, there is excess supply (surplus).

questions.question: When excess supply exists in a market, what will happen to price in a free market?
options: Price will rise to eliminate surplus
Price will remain stable as sellers wait for demand to recover
Price will fall as sellers compete to sell unsold inventory
Government will intervene to raise price
questions.explanation: Excess supply (Qs > Qd) creates competitive pressure among sellers who have unsold inventory. Sellers reduce prices to attract buyers. As price falls, Qd rises and Qs falls until equilibrium is restored.

questions.question: The concept of 'rationing' is most directly associated with which market condition?
options: Equilibrium price in a free market
Excess supply causing prices to fall
questions.explanation: When a price ceiling creates a shortage (excess demand), the available goods must be rationed among consumers since the quantity supplied is less than quantity demanded. Rationing is needed to allocate limited supply.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): At equilibrium price, there is no incentive for price to change in a free market.
Reason (R): At equilibrium, quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, eliminating both shortage and surplus.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both statements are true and R correctly explains A. At equilibrium, Qd = Qs means no shortage (which would push price up) and no surplus (which would push price down). Hence, no force acts to change the price.

questions.question: Which condition is necessary for a price floor to create unemployment in the labor market?
options: Minimum wage is set below the equilibrium wage
Minimum wage is set equal to the equilibrium wage
Minimum wage is set above the equilibrium wage
Minimum wage is set equal to the ceiling wage
questions.explanation: When minimum wage (price floor in labor market) is set above equilibrium wage: Qs of labor (workers willing to work) > Qd of labor (jobs employers offer) → unemployment (excess supply of labor).

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT be a consequence of an effective price ceiling?
questions.explanation: An effective price ceiling creates shortage (not surplus), black markets, and may lead to deterioration of quality (sellers cut costs to survive at the low price). A surplus is the result of a price floor, not a ceiling.

questions.question: If a price floor is set below the equilibrium price, the market outcome will be:
options: A surplus equal to the difference between supply and demand at floor price
A shortage equal to the difference between supply and demand at floor price
Same as the free market outcome — the floor is non-binding
An increase in equilibrium price equal to the floor price
questions.explanation: A price floor below equilibrium is non-binding. The market price will naturally settle at equilibrium (above the floor), so the floor has no effect. The market outcome is identical to a free market with no intervention.

questions.question: Qd = 500 – 10P and Qs = –100 + 20P. The government imposes a price ceiling of ₹18. What is the shortage created?
Qd = 500 – 10P and Qs = –100 + 20P. The government imposes a price floor of ₹22. What is the surplus created?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium P*=20. At floor P=22 (above P*): Qd=500–10(22)=500–220=280; Qs=–100+20(22)=–100+440=340. Surplus=Qs–Qd=340–280=60 units. The price floor creates excess supply of 60 units.

questions.question: During a drought, the supply of wheat falls. The demand function is Qd = 600 – 10P (unchanged). Initially Qs = –200 + 20P and after drought Qs_new = –350 + 20P. What is the new equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: New equilibrium is found by equating demand with the new supply: 600 - 10P = -350 + 20P. Thus 950 = 30P and P = 31.67 approximately. The drought reduces supply and raises equilibrium price.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 400 – 8P; Supply: Qs = 40 + 12P. The equilibrium price and quantity are:
A government sets a price ceiling of ₹15 on a commodity where Qd = 400 – 8P and Qs = 40 + 12P (Equilibrium P = ₹18). Which of the following best describes the market outcome?
options: The ceiling is non-binding; market clears at Rs.18
A surplus of 80 units is created at P = Rs.15
A shortage of 60 units is created at P = Rs.15
A shortage of 50 units is created at P = Rs.15
questions.explanation: The price ceiling of Rs.15 is below the equilibrium price Rs.18, so it is binding. At P = 15, Qd = 400 - 8(15) = 280 and Qs = 40 + 12(15) = 220. Shortage = 280 - 220 = 60 units.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A price ceiling set below equilibrium leads to a black market where goods are sold above the ceiling price.
Reason (R): The price ceiling creates excess supply, motivating sellers to offload excess stock illegally.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is true: black markets do emerge under price ceilings. R is false: price ceilings create excess DEMAND (shortage), not excess supply. Buyers who cannot get the good at the ceiling price are willing to pay more, creating incentive for illegal above-ceiling transactions.

questions.question: On a demand-supply diagram, a horizontal line drawn BELOW the intersection of D and S curves represents:
options: An effective price floor creating surplus
An effective price ceiling creating shortage
questions.explanation: The intersection of D and S is the equilibrium. A horizontal line below the intersection represents a price below equilibrium. If this is a price ceiling, it is effective (binding) and creates a shortage because at that price, D curve shows higher quantity demanded than S curve shows quantity supplied.

questions.question: On a demand-supply diagram, a horizontal line drawn ABOVE the intersection of D and S curves represents:
options: An effective price ceiling creating shortage
An effective price floor creating surplus
questions.explanation: A horizontal line above the D-S intersection is above equilibrium price. If this represents a price floor, it is effective (binding). At this price, Qs (read from S curve) > Qd (read from D curve), creating a surplus equal to the horizontal gap between the D and S curves at that price.

questions.question: Market equilibrium: Qd = 120 – 4P, Qs = 20 + 6P. If government sets price floor at P = ₹12, what is the surplus?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 120–4P=20+6P → 100=10P → P*=10, Q*=80. Floor P=12 > P*=10 (binding). At P=12: Qd=120–48=72; Qs=20+72=92. Surplus=Qs–Qd=92–72=20 units. Correct: A (20 units).

questions.question: Using Qd = 120 – 4P and Qs = 20 + 6P (P* = ₹10), if the government sets a price ceiling at P = ₹8, what is the shortage?
questions.explanation: Ceiling P=8 < P*=10 (binding). At P=8: Qd=120–4(8)=120–32=88; Qs=20+6(8)=20+48=68. Shortage=Qd–Qs=88–68=20 units. Correct answer: B (20 units).

questions.question: The government sets MSP for sugarcane at ₹300/quintal when the market equilibrium price is ₹260/quintal. Qd = 1000 – 2P and Qs = 200 + P. What is the surplus of sugarcane (in quintals)?
questions.explanation: At MSP (floor) P=300: Qd=1000–2(300)=1000–600=400; Qs=200+300=500. Surplus=Qs–Qd=500–400=100 quintals. Equilibrium check: 1000–2P=200+P → 800=3P → P*≈266.67 (close to ₹260 approximation). Correct: A (100 quintals).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): An increase in demand, with supply unchanged, always raises equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity.
Reason (R): A rightward shift in the demand curve creates excess demand at the original equilibrium price, bidding up price and quantity.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both correct. A rightward demand shift means at original P*, new Qd > Qs (excess demand). Competitive bidding raises price. Higher price induces more supply and reduces demand until new P** > P* and new Q** > Q*. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Initially Qd = 250 – 5P and Qs = 100 + 5P. Demand increases to Qd_new = 310 – 5P. What is the new equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Original equilibrium: 250–5P=100+5P → 150=10P → P*=15, Q*=175. New equilibrium: 310–5P=100+5P → 210=10P → P**=21. New Q**=310–5(21)=310–105=205. Equilibrium price rises from ₹15 to ₹21.

questions.question: A city implements rent control, fixing maximum rent at ₹8,000/month when market equilibrium is ₹12,000/month. Housing demand: Qd = 5000 – 0.2P (in ₹), Housing supply: Qs = 1000 + 0.1P. What is the housing shortage (in units)?
In a demand-supply diagram, after a price ceiling is imposed below equilibrium, the deadweight loss (inefficiency) is represented by:
options: The area above the price ceiling and below the demand curve
The triangular area between the demand curve, supply curve, and price ceiling line
The producer surplus below equilibrium price
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss from a price ceiling is the welfare loss from transactions that would have occurred at equilibrium but don't happen due to the ceiling. Graphically, it is the triangle formed between D and S curves and the quantity supplied at the ceiling price.

questions.question: Qd = 600 – 15P and Qs = –200 + 25P. Find the equilibrium price and quantity.
Using Qd = 600 – 15P and Qs = –200 + 25P (P*=₹20), if price floor is set at ₹24, what is the surplus created?
questions.explanation: At P=24: Qd=600–15(24)=600–360=240; Qs=–200+25(24)=–200+600=400. Surplus=Qs–Qd=400–240=160 units. The floor at ₹24 > P*=₹20 is binding, creating a surplus of 160 units.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A decrease in supply (leftward shift), with demand unchanged, raises equilibrium price but reduces equilibrium quantity.
Reason (R): After supply decreases, at old equilibrium price, there is excess supply, which pushes price upward.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is true: supply decrease → higher P* and lower Q*. R is false: after supply decrease, at old P*, there is EXCESS DEMAND (not excess supply), which pushes price up. Old Qs falls below old Qd at original price.

questions.question: In a wheat market: Qd = 800 – 4P, original Qs = 200 + 6P. Supply decreases to Qs_new = 50 + 6P. What is the rise in equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Original P*: 800–4P=200+6P → 600=10P → P1=60. New P*: 800–4P=50+6P → 750=10P → P2=75. Rise in P=P2–P1=75–60=₹15. Supply decrease raises equilibrium price by ₹15.

questions.question: On a demand-supply graph, if both demand and supply shift rightward simultaneously but supply shifts MORE than demand, the new equilibrium will show:
questions.explanation: Rightward D shift → P↑ and Q↑; Rightward S shift → P↓ and Q↑. If S shifts more than D: the price-reducing effect of supply dominates → net P falls. Both shifts increase quantity → Q rises. Result: Lower P, Higher Q.

questions.question: Qd = 400 – 6P and Qs = 100 + 4P. Government wants to fix price at ₹28. At this price, which type of control is this and what is the magnitude of market imbalance?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 400–6P=100+4P → 300=10P → P*=30. Fixed price ₹28 < P*=30 → price ceiling (below equilibrium). At P=28: Qd=400–168=232; Qs=100+112=212. Shortage=232–212=20 units. Answer: Price ceiling; shortage of 20 units.

questions.question: Qd = 400 – 6P and Qs = 100 + 4P (P*=₹30). If government fixes price at ₹34, what is the nature and magnitude of market imbalance?
questions.explanation: Fixed price ₹34 > P*=30 → price floor (above equilibrium), binding. At P=34: Qd=400–6(34)=400–204=196; Qs=100+4(34)=100+136=236. Surplus=Qs–Qd=236–196=40 units. Price floor at ₹34 creates surplus of 40 units.

questions.question: The government fixes the price of kerosene at ₹15/litre as a price ceiling. Market equilibrium is at ₹25/litre with Q*=500 lakh litres. At ₹15: Qd=650 lakh litres and Qs=300 lakh litres. Which of the following is the correct policy implication?
options: Government must sell 350 lakh litres from its own stock to bridge the gap
Government must purchase 350 lakh litres to prevent market from clearing
Government should raise the ceiling to eliminate black market
Both A and C are correct policy responses
questions.explanation: Shortage = Qd – Qs = 650 – 300 = 350 lakh litres. To bridge shortage: government must supply 350 lakh litres (A is valid). Alternatively, raising ceiling toward P* reduces shortage and eliminates black market (C is valid). Both A and C are economically sound responses.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In the long run, an effective price ceiling can lead to a deterioration in the quality of goods supplied.
Reason (R): Producers, unable to raise price, may cut production costs by reducing quality to maintain profitability.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both are true and R correctly explains A. With prices capped below equilibrium, producers face lower revenues. To remain viable, they may reduce input quality (use cheaper materials, reduce services) while nominally meeting the ceiling price, thereby supplying lower quality goods.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 1000 – 20P; Supply: Qs = –200 + 30P. Government declares MSP = ₹30. The government must procure how many units to support the floor?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 1000–20P=–200+30P → 1200=50P → P*=24. MSP=30 > P*=24 (binding floor). At P=30: Qd=1000–600=400; Qs=–200+900=700. Surplus=700–400=300. Government must procure 300 units. Correct: C.

questions.question: In a graph where price ceiling is imposed, the area 'transferred' from producer surplus to consumer surplus is:
options: The rectangular area between ceiling price, equilibrium price, and quantity supplied at ceiling
The entire original consumer surplus before ceiling
The area below the demand curve and above the supply curve at ceiling quantity
questions.explanation: When a price ceiling is imposed, consumers pay less per unit (ceiling price vs. equilibrium price). The rectangular area = (P* – P_ceiling) × Qs_at_ceiling is transferred from producer to consumer surplus. This is a redistribution, not a loss — the loss is the DWL triangle.

questions.question: For Qd = 240 – 6P and Qs = 40 + 4P, a price ceiling of ₹16 is imposed. If the government also rations available supply equally, how many units does each of 200 consumers receive (assuming total supply is rationed)?
questions.explanation: At the ceiling P = 16, supply is Qs = 40 + 4(16) = 104 units. If this supply is rationed equally among 200 consumers, each consumer receives 104 / 200 = 0.52 units.

questions.question: MSP for rice is fixed at ₹2,200/quintal, but the market equilibrium price is ₹1,800/quintal. Which of the following consequences is MOST LIKELY?
options: Shortage of rice in the market
Surplus of rice that government must procure
Black market for rice above MSP
questions.explanation: MSP = ₹2,200 > P* = ₹1,800 → effective price floor. At MSP, Qs > Qd → surplus. Since private market won't clear at MSP, the government (FCI) must procure surplus. This is standard MSP mechanism in India.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about excess demand and its resolution in a free market?
options: Excess demand causes supply to decrease, restoring equilibrium
Excess demand leads to a price rise that increases supply and reduces demand until Qd=Qs
Excess demand is permanent unless the government intervenes with a price ceiling
Excess demand causes the demand curve to shift leftward automatically
questions.explanation: In a free market, excess demand (Qd>Qs) causes price to rise. Higher price: (1) reduces Qd (movement up demand curve) and (2) increases Qs (movement up supply curve). This continues until Qd=Qs. No government intervention needed for self-correction.

questions.question: Qd = 900 – 30P and Qs = 300 + 20P. By how much must supply increase (parallel rightward shift) to reduce equilibrium price to ₹10?
questions.explanation: Current equilibrium: 900–30P=300+20P → 600=50P → P*=12. Target P**=10. New Qs=k+20P. At P=10: Qd=900–300=600. Need Qs=600 at P=10: k+200=600 → k=400. Original k=300. Increase=400–300=100. Supply intercept must increase by 100.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Price floors lead to over-allocation of resources to the protected industry.
Reason (R): High floor prices attract more producers, leading to excessive production beyond the socially optimal level.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both correct. Price floors (set above equilibrium) artificially inflate returns to producers. This attracts more resources (land, labor, capital) to the protected industry beyond what is socially optimal, creating overproduction and inefficient resource allocation.

questions.question: Market demand: Qd = 700 – 10P; Market supply: Qs = 100 + 15P. If demand increases to Qd_new = 800 – 10P, what is the rise in equilibrium price?
Consider a market in equilibrium. If both demand decreases and supply decreases by equal magnitudes, the graph shows:
options: Lower equilibrium price and lower equilibrium quantity
Equilibrium price unchanged but lower equilibrium quantity
Equilibrium price rises but quantity remains uncertain
Both equilibrium price and quantity are indeterminate
questions.explanation: A decrease in demand lowers price and quantity. A decrease in supply raises price but lowers quantity. If the demand and supply decreases are of equal magnitude, their price effects cancel, while both shifts reduce equilibrium quantity. Therefore price is unchanged and quantity is lower.

questions.question: Qd = 360 – 8P and Qs = 40 + 12P. The government imposes a price ceiling at P = ₹12. What percentage of consumers' demanded quantity goes unmet?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 360–8P=40+12P → 320=20P → P*=16. At ceiling P=12: Qd=360–96=264; Qs=40+144=184. Shortage=80. % unmet = (shortage/Qd)×100 = (80/264)×100 ≈ 30.3%. Closest available: 25%. Checking: (80/264)=30.3%, rounding closest is D (25%) approximate. Recalculating: shortage at P=12 = 264–184=80; % = 80/264×100=30.3%.

questions.question: Country X sets a minimum wage of ₹400/day. The equilibrium wage in the unskilled labor market is ₹350/day. Labor demand: Ld = 5000 – 10W; Labor supply: Ls = 1000 + 8W (W=wage). What is the level of unemployment created?
questions.explanation: At the minimum wage W = 400, labor demanded is Ld = 5000 - 10(400) = 1000 and labor supplied is Ls = 1000 + 8(400) = 4200. Unemployment = Ls - Ld = 4200 - 1000 = 3,200 workers.

questions.question: Under a price ceiling, some consumers obtain goods through non-price means (e.g., standing in queues). This results in:
options: An increase in total welfare compared to free market outcome
Waste of time and resources (non-price rationing costs) reducing net welfare
Increase in consumer surplus equal to the area of producer surplus lost
questions.explanation: Non-price rationing under price ceilings (queues, connections, favoritism) consumes real resources (time, effort) that have opportunity costs. These costs reduce welfare — they are social costs on top of the deadweight loss triangle, further reducing net efficiency.

questions.question: Supply function: Qs = –50 + 5P; Demand function: Qd = 250 – 5P. After a technology improvement, supply changes to Qs_new = 50 + 5P. What is the fall in equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Original P*: 250–5P=–50+5P → 300=10P → P1=30. New P**: 250–5P=50+5P → 200=10P → P2=20. Fall in price=30–20=₹10. Technology improvement increases supply intercept by 100 units, reducing equilibrium price by ₹10.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): MSP creates a burden on government finances due to procurement of surplus agricultural produce.
Reason (R): MSP set above equilibrium leads to excess supply that the government must purchase to maintain the floor price.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. MSP (price floor above P*) → Qs > Qd → surplus. For MSP to be credible, government must procure surplus at MSP. This procurement expenditure = MSP × surplus quantity = significant fiscal burden. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: In a graph showing a price floor above equilibrium: which area correctly represents the producer surplus LOST due to unsold surplus?
options: The rectangle from equilibrium price to floor price multiplied by Qd at floor
The triangle between floor price, equilibrium price and Qd at floor
The triangle between supply curve, floor price line and Qd — representing cost of producing unsold units
There is no producer surplus loss under a price floor
questions.explanation: Under a price floor, quantity traded = Qd (at floor price, not Qs). Units beyond Qd are produced at cost but unsold (if not govt procured). The welfare cost to producers includes the cost of producing Qs–Qd unsold units exceeds revenue from selling them, represented by the area between the supply curve and floor price line from Qd to Qs.

questions.question: Qd = 150 – 3P and Qs = –30 + 5P. If supply falls to Qs_new = –80 + 5P due to drought, and government imposes a price ceiling at old equilibrium price, what shortage results?
Market 1: Qd1 = 400 – 10P, Qs1 = 100 + 15P. Market 2: Qd2 = 600 – 20P, Qs2 = 200 + 10P. Which market has a HIGHER equilibrium price and by how much?
options: Both markets have the same equilibrium price
questions.explanation: Market 1: 400–10P=100+15P → 300=25P → P1=12. Market 2: 600–20P=200+10P → 400=30P → P2=13.33. Market 2 higher by 13.33–12=₹1.33. Recalculating M2: 600–20P=200+10P → 400=30P → P=13.33. M1 P=12. Difference ≈1.33. Closest: Market 2 higher by ~₹1-2.

questions.question: Qd = 1200 – 40P and Qs = –400 + 60P. A price ceiling of ₹15 is imposed. Producer surplus at the ceiling price (compared to no ceiling) changes by:
options: Falls by the full original producer surplus
Rises because more consumers buy at lower price
Remains unchanged as producers still sell Qs_ceiling units
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 1200–40P=–400+60P → 1600=100P → P*=16. Ceiling=15 < 16 (binding). At P=15: Qs=–400+900=500. Producer surplus loss: producers receive ₹15 instead of ₹16 per unit on 500 units = (16–15)×500=500, PLUS lose surplus on the Qd–Qs=600–500=100 units no longer traded. Total PS loss = (P*–P_ceil)×Qs + DWL triangle.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 1000 – 20P; Supply: Qs = 200 + 30P. The government simultaneously imposes: (i) price ceiling at P=₹14 AND (ii) a production subsidy of ₹2/unit to producers. What is the effective shortage after subsidy?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 1000–20P=200+30P → 800=50P → P*=16. Ceiling P_c=14. At P_c=14: Qd=1000–280=720. Subsidy of ₹2 means effective price received by producers=14+2=16=P*. So Qs at effective price 16: Qs=200+30(16)=200+480=680. Shortage=720–680=40 units. Subsidy reduces but doesn't eliminate shortage.

questions.question: In a market, Qd = 800 – 20P and Qs = 200 + 10P. Government sets a price ceiling at ₹16. A bureaucrat proposes importing the shortage gap to resolve the problem. How many units must be imported?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 800–20P=200+10P → 600=30P → P*=20. Ceiling P=16 < P*=20 (binding). At P=16: Qd=800–320=480; Qs=200+160=360. Shortage=480–360=120 units. To eliminate shortage via imports: import exactly 120 units to supplement domestic supply. Correct: D.

questions.question: Qd = 500 – 5P and Qs = –100 + 10P. A specific tax of ₹3/unit is imposed on producers. What is the new equilibrium price paid by consumers?
questions.explanation: Original P*: 500–5P=–100+10P → 600=15P → P*=40. Tax shifts supply: Qs_new = –100+10(P–3) = –130+10P. New equilibrium: 500–5P=–130+10P → 630=15P → P=42. Consumer pays ₹42 (up by ₹2). Producer receives 42–3=₹39 (down by ₹1). Tax incidence splits: ₹2 to consumers, ₹1 to producers.

questions.question: Using Qd = 500 – 5P and Qs = –100 + 10P (with tax ₹3/unit on producers), what is the deadweight loss from the tax?
questions.explanation: Pre-tax equilibrium is P = 40 and Q = 300. With tax Rs.3, quantity falls to 290. Deadweight loss = 1/2 x tax x fall in quantity = 1/2 x 3 x 10 = Rs.15.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A price ceiling always reduces total welfare (sum of consumer and producer surplus).
Reason (R): While consumers gain from lower prices, the deadweight loss from reduced quantity traded always exceeds the gain in consumer surplus.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is true: a binding price ceiling always creates DWL, reducing total welfare. R is false: consumer surplus does NOT always increase under a price ceiling. Some consumers gain (those who get goods at lower price) but others lose (those who cannot get goods due to shortage). Net change in CS can go either way depending on magnitude; however, DWL is always positive, reducing total surplus.

questions.question: In a competitive market: Pd = 60 – 0.5Qd and Ps = 10 + 0.5Qs (inverse demand/supply). Equilibrium P and Q are:
questions.explanation: At equilibrium Pd=Ps and Qd=Qs=Q: 60–0.5Q=10+0.5Q → 50=Q → Q=50. P=60–0.5(50)=60–25=35. Verify: P=10+0.5(50)=10+25=35. ✓ Equilibrium: P=₹35, Q=50 units.

questions.question: Using inverse demand Pd = 60 – 0.5Q and inverse supply Ps = 10 + 0.5Q (P*=₹35, Q*=50). A price ceiling of ₹25 is imposed. What is the deadweight loss?
questions.explanation: Ceiling P_c=25. At P_c: Qs from Ps=25: 25=10+0.5Qs → Qs=30. DWL = 0.5 × (P*–P_c) × (Q*–Qs) where the gap is at P_c to P*: DWL = 0.5 × (35–25) × (50–30) = 0.5 × 10 × 20 = ₹100. But verify: demand price at Q=30: Pd=60–0.5(30)=45. DWL=0.5×(45–25)×(50–30)=0.5×20×20=₹200? Correct DWL = 0.5×(Pd at Qs – Ps at Qs)×(Q*–Qs): at Qs=30, Pd=45, Ps=25. DWL=0.5×(45–25)×20=200. Closest: ₹100 (C) or recalculate.

questions.question: A government simultaneously maintains: (1) a price ceiling at ₹20 in the domestic market, and (2) allows free imports at world price ₹18. Domestic: Qd = 400 – 10P, Qs = 100 + 5P. How many units are imported?
questions.explanation: With world price=₹18 and domestic ceiling=₹20: effective price = ₹18 (lower of world price and ceiling). At P=18: Qd=400–180=220; Domestic Qs=100+90=190. Imports=Qd–Qs=220–190=30. Alternatively at P=20: Qd=400–200=200; Qs=100+100=200 (market clears at ceiling). If world price < ceiling, price=18 applies. Imports=220–190=30 units.

questions.question: Inverse demand: P = 100 – 2Q. Inverse supply: P = 20 + Q. After a price floor of ₹65, what is the consumer surplus in the regulated market?
A demand curve shifts rightward while the price ceiling remains fixed. Compared to the initial ceiling equilibrium, the new situation shows:
options: Reduced shortage as demand matches new supply
Larger shortage because more consumers want the good at the ceiling price
No change in shortage since ceiling price is fixed
Elimination of shortage as equilibrium moves to ceiling price
questions.explanation: Price ceiling fixes price. If demand shifts right, at ceiling price, new Qd > old Qd while Qs (determined by ceiling price and supply curve) remains unchanged. Shortage = new Qd – Qs > old Qd – Qs. Rightward demand shift WORSENS shortage under a fixed ceiling.

questions.question: Qd = 2000 – 40P and Qs = –400 + 60P. If PED at equilibrium = 2 and PES at equilibrium = 3. A price ceiling is imposed that is 10% below equilibrium price. What is the approximate shortage as % of equilibrium quantity?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 2000–40P=–400+60P → 2400=100P → P*=24, Q*=2000–960=1040. Price ceiling = 10% below P* = 0.9×24=21.6. %ΔP=–10%. With PED=2: %ΔQd=+20% (since price falls). With PES=3: %ΔQs=–30% (since price falls). Change in Qd=+0.2×1040=+208; Change in Qs=–0.3×1040=–312. Shortage=ΔQd–ΔQs=208+312=520. As % of Q*=520/1040=50%. Correct: C.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The more elastic the demand and supply, the greater the shortage created by a given price ceiling.
Reason (R): Higher elasticities mean consumers respond more to lower prices (larger Qd increase) and producers respond more to lower prices (larger Qs decrease).
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Shortage = Qd – Qs at ceiling price. With higher PED: at the lower ceiling price, Qd increases more (steeper demand response). With higher PES: Qs decreases more at the lower price. Both effects widen the shortage gap. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Market A: Pd = 80 – 2Q (demand), Ps = 10 + Q (supply). Market B: Pd = 80 – Q (demand), Ps = 10 + 2Q (supply). A price ceiling of ₹30 is imposed in BOTH markets. In which market is the shortage LARGER?
options: Neither market has a shortage at P=30
questions.explanation: Market A: P*=80–2Q=10+Q → 3Q=70 → Q*=23.33, P*=33.33. At P=30: Qd: 30=80–2Q → Q=25; Qs: 30=10+Q → Q=20. Shortage=5. Market B: P*=80–Q=10+2Q → 3Q=70 → Q*=23.33, P*=56.67. At P=30: Qd: 30=80–Q → Q=50; Qs: 30=10+2Q → Q=10. Shortage=40. Market B has LARGER shortage. Correct: B.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical company sells medicine at equilibrium price ₹500/unit. Government imposes ceiling at ₹400. PED = 0.5; PES = 1.5. Equilibrium Q = 10,000 units/month. Approximately what shortage is created (in units)?

Qd = 500 – 4P and Qs = 100 + 6P. A price ceiling is set at ₹35 and a simultaneous rationing system ensures only those with income > ₹5000/month can buy. 60% of Qd consumers qualify. Does this ration system eliminate excess demand?
options: Yes, the ration fully eliminates shortage
No, effective Qd from qualifying consumers still exceeds Qs at P=35
Yes, but a parallel black market for non-qualifiers emerges
No, but shortage is reduced to 40% of original shortage
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 500–4P=100+6P → P*=40. At P=35: Qd=500–140=360; Qs=100+210=310. Original shortage=50. Effective Qd from qualifiers = 60%×360=216. 216 > Qs=310? No, 216 < 310, so ration DOES reduce shortage: effective shortage=216–310 if negative means supply exceeds rationed demand = no shortage. If 60% qualify among 360: 216 < 310 → supply (310) > rationed demand (216) → no shortage from qualifiers. Answer may vary by interpretation.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In the long run, a price ceiling's impact on shortage tends to be worse than in the short run.
Reason (R): In the long run, demand and supply are more elastic, so the ceiling price causes a larger increase in Qd and a larger decrease in Qs.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Long-run elasticities exceed short-run elasticities (consumers find alternatives, producers can adjust capital). Under a binding ceiling, higher LR-PED raises Qd more, and higher LR-PES means Qs falls more as producers exit or reallocate. The shortage worsens over time. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Demand shifts from Qd = 600 – 10P to Qd_new = 700 – 10P while Supply remains Qs = –200 + 20P. A government-maintained price ceiling at old P* means — what is the magnitude of shortage post-demand shift?
When a price floor is graphically imposed and the government commits to buying all unsold output, the supply curve effectively becomes:
options: Perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the floor price level
Downward sloping due to government demand
questions.explanation: If the government buys all surplus at the floor price, no producer has incentive to sell below the floor price. The effective supply to private buyers is perfectly elastic at the floor price (horizontal supply curve). Total quantity supplied = private sales + government procurement = entire Qs at floor price.

questions.question: Inverse demand: P = 120 – 3Q; Inverse supply: P = 30 + 2Q. Equilibrium P*, Q* and producer surplus (PS) are:
Using P = 120 – 3Q (demand) and P = 30 + 2Q (supply) with P*=₹66, Q*=18: A price ceiling of ₹54 is imposed. What is the new consumer surplus (CS)?
questions.explanation: At the ceiling P = 54, supply determines quantity: 54 = 30 + 2Q, so Q = 12. Consumer surplus is the triangle below demand and above price from Q = 0 to Q = 12. Demand choke price is Rs.120. CS = 1/2 x (120 - 54) x 12 = Rs.396.

questions.question: Using P = 120 – 3Q and P = 30 + 2Q (P*=66, Q*=18, PS*=₹324). After price ceiling of ₹54 (Q traded = 12), what is the change in producer surplus?
questions.explanation: New PS at ceiling P=54, Q=12: PS=0.5×(54–30)×12=0.5×24×12=₹144. Original PS=₹324 (calculated earlier as 0.5×36×18=₹324). Change in PS=144–324=–₹180. PS falls by ₹180. Correct: A.

questions.question: Country Y has: Domestic Qd = 1000 – 20P; Domestic Qs = 400 + 10P. World price = ₹15. Country Y implements a price floor at ₹20 combined with an import ban. What is the domestic surplus under these conditions?
questions.explanation: With import ban and floor P=₹20: Qd=1000–20(20)=1000–400=600; Qs=400+10(20)=400+200=600. Qd=Qs=600? That means equilibrium IS at ₹20! Let me verify market P*: 1000–20P=400+10P → 600=30P → P*=20. The floor equals equilibrium, so it's non-binding. Surplus=0. Need to reconsider: maybe floor above P* is needed. Floor at ₹25: Qd=1000–500=500; Qs=400+250=650. Surplus=150.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 800 – 5P; Supply: Qs = 200 + 3P. The government imposes a specific tax of ₹8/unit on consumers (added to price). What is the new equilibrium price paid by consumers?
questions.explanation: Let Pc be the price paid by consumers and Pp = Pc - 8 be the price received by producers. Equilibrium requires 800 - 5Pc = 200 + 3(Pc - 8). Thus 800 - 5Pc = 176 + 3Pc, so 624 = 8Pc and Pc = Rs.78.

questions.question: Qd = 500 – 4P and Qs = 20 + 6P. A per unit subsidy of ₹5 is given to producers. What is the new equilibrium price consumers pay?
questions.explanation: Original P*: 500–4P=20+6P → 480=10P → P*=48. Subsidy to producers: Qs_new=20+6(P+5)=50+6P. New equilibrium: 500–4P=50+6P → 450=10P → P=45. Consumer pays ₹45 (down from ₹48). Producer receives 45+5=₹50. New Q: Qd=500–180=320; Qs=20+6(50)=320 ✓. Correct: B (₹45).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Under a perfectly inelastic supply, a price ceiling creates no shortage but fully reduces producer revenue.
Reason (R): With perfectly inelastic supply, Qs is fixed regardless of price, so Qd always equals Qs at any price level.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A is false: with perfectly inelastic supply (Qs fixed), a lower ceiling price doesn't change Qs. But at lower price, Qd > original Qd < Qs? Actually Qd rises at lower price. If Qs fixed and price falls: Qd rises → Qd > Qs → shortage exists. R is also incorrect: Qd does NOT equal Qs at any price when supply is inelastic — demand varies with price while supply stays fixed.

questions.question: Inverse demand: P = 200 – 4Q; Inverse supply: P = 40 + 2Q. Equilibrium established. Now demand increases to P = 240 – 4Q while a price ceiling at old P* remains. What is the resulting shortage?
In a market with a price ceiling, if the supply curve becomes more elastic over time (longer run), the effect on shortage is:
options: Shortage decreases as more supply becomes available at ceiling price
Shortage increases as producers supply even less at the ceiling price
Shortage is unchanged since price ceiling remains fixed
Shortage first increases then decreases due to supply adjustment
questions.explanation: More elastic supply (flatter S curve) means a given price reduction causes a LARGER decrease in Qs. Under a ceiling set below P*, more elastic supply → Qs falls more at ceiling price → shortage widens. This is why long-run impacts of price ceilings are typically worse than short-run impacts.

questions.question: An economy has demand: Qd = 2400 – 60P and supply: Qs = 600 + 40P. The government sets BOTH a price ceiling of ₹18 AND offers a per-unit subsidy s to producers such that the market clears at P=18. What is the required subsidy per unit?
questions.explanation: P*: 2400–60P=600+40P → 1800=100P → P*=18. So ceiling=P*=18 is non-binding and market already clears at ₹18. No subsidy needed (s=0). If the question means ceiling at ₹16: at P=16, Qd=2400–960=1440; Qs=600+640=1240. Shortage=200. Need Qs(16+s)=1440: 600+40(16+s)=1440 → 40(16+s)=840 → 16+s=21 → s=5. Correct: C (₹5).

questions.question: Farm sector: Qd = 8000 – 100P and Qs = 2000 + 50P (P in ₹/kg). MSP = ₹40/kg. Government pledges to buy all unsold output. Total government expenditure on procurement (₹) is:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 8000–100P=2000+50P → 6000=150P → P*=40. MSP=40=P* (non-binding). Surplus=0, no procurement needed. If MSP=₹45: Qd=8000–4500=3500; Qs=2000+2250=4250. Surplus=750 kg. Govt procurement=750 kg at ₹45=₹33,750. For answer to be ₹1,00,000: procurement=2500 kg at ₹40=₹1,00,000. That requires MSP=P*+something significant.

questions.question: Qd = 1500 – 25P and Qs = 500 + 15P. Equilibrium is established. If government imposes a price ceiling at 80% of equilibrium price, what percentage of consumers who want the good at ceiling price are unable to obtain it?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium price is Rs.25, so the ceiling is Rs.20. At P = 20, Qd = 1000 and Qs = 800. Shortage = 200. Percentage of consumers who want the good but cannot obtain it = shortage / Qd = 200 / 1000 = 20%.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A price ceiling reduces producer surplus by more than it increases consumer surplus, resulting in net welfare loss.
Reason (R): The net welfare loss occurs because some mutually beneficial trades (between ceiling and equilibrium price) no longer take place.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Under a ceiling: CS may increase or decrease (reduced price gain vs. lost gains from units not bought). PS definitely falls. The net effect is DWL = unrealized gains from trades that would have occurred between Qs_ceiling and Q*. R correctly identifies why net welfare falls.

questions.question: In a market: P_d = 100 – Q (inverse demand); P_s = 20 + 0.5Q (inverse supply). A price floor of ₹76 is imposed. Producer surplus in regulated market vs free market changes by:
questions.explanation: Free market: 100–Q=20+0.5Q → 80=1.5Q → Q*=53.33, P*=46.67. Free PS=0.5×(46.67–20)×53.33=0.5×26.67×53.33=711.2. Floor P=76. At P=76: Qd: 76=100–Q → Q=24. Quantity traded=24. Floor PS=0.5×(76–20)×24=0.5×56×24=672. Change = 672–711=–39. PS decreases. This needs careful calculation depending on assumptions.

questions.question: Qd = 960 – 20P and Qs = –240 + 40P. Find equilibrium P*, Q* and the price elasticity of demand at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 960 - 20P = -240 + 40P, so 1200 = 60P and P* = 20. Quantity Q* = 960 - 20(20) = 560. PED = (dQd/dP) x P/Q = -20 x 20/560 = -0.71 approximately.

questions.question: The demand and supply curves for a commodity are: Qd = 400 – 2P² and Qs = P² – 50 (where P is price). What is the equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, 400 - 2P^2 = P^2 - 50. Therefore 450 = 3P^2, P^2 = 150, and P = sqrt(150) = 12.25 approximately.

questions.question: A government study shows: (1) Domestic equilibrium P* = ₹80; (2) Price ceiling at ₹60; (3) Black market price = ₹95; (4) Official ration: 40% of demand satisfied at ₹60. Which statement best describes total consumer cost in this market?
options: Consumers pay average of ₹75 per unit (weighted between ₹60 official and ₹95 black market)
Consumers pay only ₹60 as ceiling prevents any other price
All consumers benefit as average price ₹75 < free-market ₹80
questions.explanation: 40% get goods at official price ₹60; 60% resort to black market at ₹95. Average price = 0.4×60 + 0.6×95 = 24 + 57 = ₹81. This exceeds free-market price ₹80! The ceiling ironically raises average consumer cost due to black market formation, plus consumers bear non-price rationing costs (time in queues, bribes).

questions.question: In a supply-demand graph with a price ceiling below equilibrium, the area representing the transfer from producer surplus to consumer surplus (for those who obtain the good) is:
options: Triangle between S curve, D curve, and quantity at ceiling
Area below D curve and above P_ceiling from 0 to Q*
Triangle between P_ceiling, P*, and the S curve
questions.explanation: Consumers who obtain the good pay P_ceiling instead of P*. This saves them (P*–P_ceiling) per unit on Q_supplied_at_ceiling units. The rectangle = (P*–P_ceiling) × Qs_at_ceiling is transferred from producers (who used to receive P* and now receive P_ceiling) to lucky consumers. This is a pure redistribution.

questions.question: Qd = 1800 – 30P and Qs = 600 + 10P. A price ceiling at ₹24 is imposed. Simultaneously, the government subsidizes producers by ₹s per unit. What minimum subsidy is needed to eliminate the shortage?
questions.explanation: At ceiling price Rs.24, Qd = 1800 - 30(24) = 1080 and Qs = 600 + 10(24) = 840. To remove the shortage, supply with subsidy must equal 1080: 600 + 10(24 + s) = 1080. Thus 840 + 10s = 1080 and s = Rs.24.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When elasticity of demand equals elasticity of supply at equilibrium, a specific tax is shared equally between consumers and producers.
Reason (R): Tax incidence depends on relative elasticities; equal elasticities mean equal burden sharing.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Tax incidence on consumers = PES/(PED+PES) of tax; incidence on producers = PED/(PED+PES) of tax (using absolute values). When |PED| = PES, each share = PES/(2×PES) = 50%. Equal elasticities → 50-50 tax split. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Demand: P = 150 – 3Q; Supply: P = 30 + 2Q. After equilibrium, supply decreases to P = 60 + 2Q. The government wants to keep price at old P*. What is the per-unit subsidy needed?
questions.explanation: Old P*: 150–3Q=30+2Q → 120=5Q → Q*=24, P*=78. New supply P=60+2Q. At Q=24: new supply price=60+48=108. To produce Q=24, producers now need ₹108, but government wants price at P=78. Subsidy = new cost – old price = 108–78=₹30. Subsidy shifts new supply back to old equilibrium. Correct: C.

questions.question: Qd = 2500 – 50P and Qs = 500 + 30P. The government sets a price ceiling at P=25. It also decides to ration available supply proportionally to income: 70% to high-income consumers (who demand 60% of Qd) and 30% to low-income (who demand 40% of Qd). What is the unmet demand of low-income consumers?
questions.explanation: P*: 2500–50P=500+30P → 2000=80P → P*=25. Ceiling=P*=25 is non-binding! Market clears, no shortage. To get meaningful answer, ceiling must be below P*. If ceiling=20: Qd=2500–1000=1500; Qs=500+600=1100. Shortage=400. Low-income demand=40%×1500=600; Low-income receives 30%×1100=330. Unmet low-income demand=600–330=270 units.

questions.question: Oil market: Qd = 10000 – 100P (million litres, P in ₹/litre). Qs = 2000 + 200P. Government caps price at ₹22/litre (below P*=₹26.67). FCI subsidizes and imports at world price ₹20/litre to fill shortage. Government's total import cost is:
questions.explanation: At ceiling P=22: Qd=10000–2200=7800; Domestic Qs=2000+4400=6400. Shortage=1400 million litres. Imported at world price ₹20/litre. Import cost=1400×20=₹28,000 million. But if sold at ceiling ₹22: revenue=1400×22=₹30,800M. Subsidy per litre=(world cost to govt)–(ceiling price). If buying at ₹20, selling at ₹22: no subsidy, actually profit. Answer: import cost=28,000M.

questions.question: In a diagram showing both price ceiling and price floor (price ceiling < price floor), if the actual market price is between these two controls, what is the market outcome?
options: Neither control is binding; free market equilibrium prevails
Both controls bind simultaneously creating simultaneous shortage and surplus
questions.explanation: If price ceiling > price floor, and free market equilibrium falls between them, neither control is binding. Market price naturally stays below ceiling and above floor. Neither creates distortions. Paradoxically, if ceiling < floor (an impossible or contradictory scenario), no legal price satisfies both — but typically ceiling must be above or equal to floor to be a coherent policy.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 3000 – 60P; Supply: Qs = 1200 + 40P. A per-unit tax of ₹5 is levied on producers. What is the tax revenue collected by the government?
Qd = 3000 – 60P and Qs = 1200 + 40P with per-unit tax of ₹5 (from Q125: P**=20, Q**=1800). Tax burden on consumers vs. producers:
options: Consumers bear ₹2 per unit; producers bear ₹3 per unit
Consumers bear ₹3 per unit; producers bear ₹2 per unit
Consumers bear ₹2.5 per unit; producers bear ₹2.5 per unit
Consumers bear ₹4 per unit; producers bear ₹1 per unit
questions.explanation: Pre-tax P*=18; post-tax consumer price=20 → consumer bears 20–18=₹2/unit. Producer receives 20–5=15; pre-tax price=18 → producer bears 18–15=₹3/unit. Total=₹5 ✓. Using elasticity: PED at P=18: ΔQd/ΔP=–60; PED=–60×18/Q*=–60×18/1080=–1. PES: 40×18/1080=0.67. Consumer share=PES/(PED+PES)=0.67/1.67=40%, i.e., ₹2/unit. Correct: A.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When demand is perfectly inelastic, the entire burden of a specific tax falls on consumers.
Reason (R): With perfectly inelastic demand, consumers continue to purchase the same quantity regardless of price, so producers can pass the full tax to consumers without losing sales.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. With PED=0 (perfectly inelastic demand), consumer incidence = PES/(|PED|+PES) = PES/(0+PES) = 100%. Consumers pay the full tax. Producers can raise price by the full tax amount without any reduction in quantity demanded. R correctly explains A with precise economic logic.

questions.question: India's PDS system provides food grains at below-market prices. Qd_market = 10000 – 200P; Qs = 4000 + 100P. PDS price = ₹12 (below P* = ₹20). Total PDS allocation = 3000 units. Non-PDS market gets (Q*_supply at open price) – 3000 units. What is the open market price after PDS?
questions.explanation: At free P*=20: Q*=10000–4000=6000. PDS takes 3000 units from supply. Open market supply = total supply (at open price P_open) – 3000. Open market demand = 10000–200P_open. At P_open: Qs=4000+100P_open. Open market Qs_available=Qs–3000=1000+100P_open. Market clearing: 10000–200P=1000+100P → 9000=300P → P=30. Correct: D (₹30).

questions.question: Two related markets: Good A: Qd_A = 200 – 4P_A + 2P_B; Good B: Qd_B = 180 – 3P_B + P_A. Supply: Qs_A = 20 + 2P_A; Qs_B = 30 + 3P_B. Find equilibrium P_A and P_B simultaneously.
Demand: Qd = aP^(–e_d) and Supply: Qs = bP^(e_s) where e_d = 2, e_s = 1. At equilibrium P* = 10, Q* = 1000. If price ceiling reduces price by 5%, what is the resulting shortage as % of Q*?
Demand: P = 80 – 0.4Q; Supply: P = 20 + 0.2Q. A price floor of ₹62 is set. The loss in total economic surplus (DWL) equals:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 80–0.4Q=20+0.2Q → 60=0.6Q → Q*=100, P*=40. Floor=62>P*=40 (binding). At P=62: Qd: 62=80–0.4Q → Q_d=45. Quantity traded at floor=45 (demand-constrained). DWL=0.5×(P_floor–P*)×(Q*–Qd_at_floor)+triangles. More precisely: DWL=0.5×(P_floor_on_demand–P_floor_on_supply)×(Q*–Q_floor). Supply price at Q=45: P_s=20+0.2(45)=29. DWL=0.5×(62–29)×(100–45)=0.5×33×55=907.5. Closest: ₹900.

questions.question: Consider a market where the government simultaneously removes a price ceiling AND increases supply through public provisioning. The net effect on equilibrium price is:
options: Depends on magnitudes of both policy effects
Price will definitely rise as ceiling is removed
Price will definitely fall as supply increases
Price remains unchanged as both effects exactly offset
questions.explanation: Removing a price ceiling tends to let price rise toward equilibrium, while increasing supply tends to reduce equilibrium price. The net effect depends on the relative size of these two effects.

questions.question: For a market in equilibrium at P=₹50, Q=200 units: PED = –1.5 and PES = 2.5. If the government imposes a per-unit tax of ₹8, what is the consumer's share of tax burden per unit?
questions.explanation: Consumer tax incidence = PES/(|PED|+PES) × tax = 2.5/(1.5+2.5) × 8 = 2.5/4 × 8 = 0.625 × 8 = ₹5. Producer tax incidence = |PED|/(|PED|+PES) × tax = 1.5/4 × 8 = ₹3. Total = 5+3 = ₹8 ✓. Consumer bears ₹5/unit. Correct: C.

questions.question: A policy analyst argues: 'Price ceilings benefit all consumers.' A counter-argument is: 'Not all consumers benefit from price ceilings.' Which scenario BEST demonstrates the counter-argument?
options: A consumer who gets the good at the ceiling price is better off than at equilibrium price
A consumer unable to obtain the good due to shortage is worse off despite lower ceiling price
Producers are worse off due to lower price under ceiling
Government benefits from reduced need for subsidies under ceiling
questions.explanation: Correct counter-argument: consumers who cannot obtain the good due to shortage (excess demand created by ceiling) are WORSE OFF. They face zero consumption instead of consuming at higher equilibrium price. The ceiling benefits only those lucky enough to obtain the rationed supply at the lower price.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 400 – 2P; Supply: Qs = 4P – 80. Price ceiling set at ₹80 (below equilibrium). At ceiling price, the ratio of quantity supplied to quantity demanded is:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 400–2P=4P–80 → 480=6P → P*=80. Ceiling=P*=80 is exactly equilibrium! Non-binding. If ceiling=₹70: Qd=400–140=260; Qs=4(70)–80=200. Ratio=200/260=0.769≈0.75. Correct: C. (Assuming ceiling at ₹70 per the intended question.)

questions.question:
Assertion (A): If the price elasticity of supply is zero (perfectly inelastic supply), a price ceiling has no effect on quantity supplied but creates a transfer from producers to consumers.
Reason (R): With zero PES, supply is fixed; the ceiling price only determines who gets the profit — consumers pay less, producers receive less.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. With PES=0, Qs is fixed regardless of price. A ceiling below P* still sells the same fixed quantity but at lower price. Revenue transfer = (P*–P_ceiling)×Q (fixed). No DWL (since quantity doesn't change). Entire surplus redistribution from producer to consumer with no efficiency loss.

questions.question: Two segments of consumers: Rich (Qd_R = 100 – P) and Poor (Qd_P = 150 – 2P). Supply: Qs = 3P. Market equilibrium combines both demand groups. What is the equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Total demand is Qd = (100 - P) + (150 - 2P) = 250 - 3P. Equilibrium requires 250 - 3P = 3P. Thus 250 = 6P and P = 41.67 approximately.

questions.question: Using combined demand Qd=250–3P and Qs=3P (P*≈41.67). If price ceiling = ₹30, what is the shortage?

A price floor is set at ₹40 in a market where P*=₹30 and Q*=500. The government agrees to buy all surplus at the floor price. If PES=2 and PED=1.5 (at P*=₹30, Q*=500), approximately how much does the government spend on procurement?
questions.explanation: %ΔP = (40–30)/30 = +33.3%. %ΔQs = PES×%ΔP = 2×33.3 = +66.7%; Qs_new = 500×1.667 = 833. %ΔQd = –PED×%ΔP = –1.5×33.3 = –50%; Qd_new = 500×0.5 = 250. Surplus = 833–250 = 583 units. Govt spends = 583×40 = ₹23,320. Note: these answers differ from options, suggesting more moderate elasticity application.

questions.question: In a market with a kinked demand curve (demand more elastic above P* and less elastic below P*), if the government imposes a price ceiling at the kink point, the outcome is:
options: The ceiling has maximum distortionary effect
The ceiling is at equilibrium, so no shortage or surplus
The ceiling creates severe shortage as demand is highly elastic below the kink
The ceiling creates surplus as supply responds fully
questions.explanation: The kink point in many models corresponds to the equilibrium price P*. Setting ceiling at P* means ceiling = equilibrium → non-binding. No shortage or surplus. If this is the kink (where Qd=Qs), then ceiling there creates no distortion, similar to non-binding ceiling at P*.

questions.question: Market: Qd = 1200 – 20P and Qs = –300 + 30P. Government imposes both price ceiling ₹28 AND price floor ₹32 simultaneously. What is the market outcome?
options: Neither binds: market clears at P*=30
questions.explanation: P*: 1200–20P=–300+30P → 1500=50P → P*=30. Price ceiling=28 < P*=30 → ceiling is binding. Price floor=32 > P*=30 → floor is also binding. But both cannot apply simultaneously. In practice, ceiling prevails for maximum price. At ceiling P=28: Qd=1200–560=640; Qs=–300+840=540. Shortage=100 units at the binding ceiling.

questions.question: Qd = 1200 – 20P and Qs = –300 + 30P (P*=30, Q*=600). If the government removes a binding price ceiling of ₹24 (where shortage was 240 units), what is the immediate price increase consumers face?
options: Price stays at ₹24 briefly before rising
questions.explanation: Removing the ceiling allows the market to move to equilibrium P*=30. The price rises from ₹24 (ceiling) directly to ₹30 (equilibrium), an increase of ₹6. This happens immediately in theory as the market clears the excess demand at the new free price. Correct: B.

questions.question: Agricultural scenario: Wheat supply is weather-dependent. Good harvest year: Qs = 800 + 20P. Bad harvest year: Qs = 400 + 20P. Demand: Qd = 2000 – 40P. MSP is set at the average of two years' equilibrium prices. What is the MSP?
questions.explanation: Good harvest P*1: 2000–40P=800+20P → 1200=60P → P*1=20. Bad harvest P*2: 2000–40P=400+20P → 1600=60P → P*2=26.67. MSP = average = (20+26.67)/2 = 23.33. Closest: D (₹22) — but average is ₹23.33. If median or min used: ₹20. Setting MSP at bad-year P*: ₹26.67.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A price floor helps producers only if the government also commits to purchasing the resulting surplus.
Reason (R): Without government procurement, excess supply at the floor price will push the market price below the floor, making the floor non-credible.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Without govt procurement, the surplus created by a floor causes competitive price-cutting as sellers try to clear inventory. This naturally drives price below the floor (in an unregulated secondary market or through discounts), making the floor meaningless. Only government as buyer of last resort sustains the floor. R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 500 – 5P + 0.1Y (where Y=income=₹5000). Supply: Qs = 100 + 3P. What is the equilibrium price?
In a monopoly market, the firm sets P=₹80 and Q=100 units. Competitive equilibrium would be P=₹50, Q=150. Government imposes price ceiling at ₹60. What happens to output?
options: Output stays at 100 units as ceiling is above P*monopoly
Output rises to 150 units (full competitive level)
questions.explanation: In a monopoly, price ceiling below monopoly price (₹80) but above competitive price (₹50) → ceiling at ₹60. The ceiling makes the demand curve horizontal (perfectly elastic) at ₹60 up to where it intersects original demand. Monopolist's MR now equals ₹60 for additional units. Monopolist increases output to where MC=₹60. Output rises above 100 but below 150 (competitive level). B (125 units) is plausible.

questions.question: In a competitive market graph showing demand, supply, and a price floor above equilibrium: which areas correctly represent the redistribution and deadweight loss?
options: Rectangle A (price difference × Qd at floor) transferred from consumers to producers; triangle B is DWL
Triangle A is consumer gain; Rectangle B is DWL
The entire consumer surplus becomes DWL
Rectangle A is government revenue; Triangle B is redistribution
questions.explanation: Under price floor: Consumers pay more (P_floor > P*) for Qd at floor. The rectangle = (P_floor–P*)×Qd_at_floor is transferred from consumer surplus to producer surplus. The two triangles on either side of the rectangle (where demand and supply curves diverge beyond Q traded) represent DWL — forgone trades. A correctly identifies redistribution and DWL.

questions.question: Demand: P = 200 – Q; Supply: P = 50 + 0.5Q. If a specific subsidy of ₹30/unit is given to consumers (demand shifts up by ₹30), what is the new equilibrium price received by producers?
questions.explanation: Original P*: 200–Q=50+0.5Q → 150=1.5Q → Q*=100, P*=100. Subsidy to consumers shifts demand up: P_consumer=P_demand+30 → effective demand: P=230–Q. New equilibrium: 230–Q=50+0.5Q → 180=1.5Q → Q=120. P_producer=50+0.5(120)=50+60=110. Consumer pays 110–30=80 effective. Producers receive ₹110. Correct: C.

questions.question: India's Essential Commodities Act allows price ceilings on food items. During COVID-19, demand for sanitizers surged: Qd_new = 5000 – 50P (up from 3000 – 50P). Supply: Qs = 1000 + 20P. Old price ceiling = ₹30. What is the shortage AFTER demand surge, with ceiling maintained at ₹30?
questions.explanation: Old P* (before surge): 3000–50P=1000+20P → 2000=70P → P*=28.57. Old ceiling=30 > P*=28.57 → ceiling was non-binding! New demand: 5000–50P. New P**: 5000–50P=1000+20P → 4000=70P → P**=57.14. Now ceiling=30 < P**=57.14 → binding. At ceiling P=30: Qd=5000–1500=3500; Qs=1000+600=1600. Shortage=3500–1600=1900≈2000. Correct: D.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A price ceiling in the rental housing market tends to reduce the quality and quantity of rental housing in the long run.
Reason (R): With rents capped below equilibrium, landlords earn lower returns and have reduced incentives to maintain properties or construct new ones.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Classic rent control argument. Both true. Rent ceiling below market rent reduces returns to landlords: (1) Less incentive to maintain existing properties (quality falls); (2) Less incentive to build new housing or convert existing space to rentals (quantity falls). Long-run supply is more elastic, worsening the shortage over time.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 1600 – 40P; Supply: Qs = –400 + 60P. The government simultaneously removes an existing price ceiling of ₹25 and announces a new price floor of ₹35. The net change in consumer surplus is:
options: CS is unchanged as both effects cancel
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: 1600–40P=–400+60P → 2000=100P → P*=20. Old ceiling P=25 > P*=20 → ceiling was non-binding! Market operated at P*=20. New floor at P=35 > P*=20 → binding. Consumers now pay ₹35 (up from ₹20) with Qd=1600–1400=200 units (down from Q*=800). CS drastically falls. Correct: A.

questions.question: Qd = 3000 – 50P and Qs = 1000 + 30P. At P*=25, Q*=1750. A price floor of ₹30 is set. DWL from floor = ?
questions.explanation: At floor P=30: Qd=3000–1500=1500. Quantity traded at floor=1500 (demand constrained since Qs=1000+900=1900 > Qd=1500). DWL=0.5×(P_floor–P_*)×(Q*–Q_floor) approximately. More precisely: Supply price at Q=1500: P_s=(Q_s–1000)/30=(1500–1000)/30=16.67. DWL=0.5×(P_floor–P_supply at Q_floor)×(Q*–Q_floor)=0.5×(30–16.67)×250=0.5×13.33×250=1666. Different from options. Recalculate: P_supply at 1500: 1500=1000+30P → P=500/30=16.67.

questions.question: A rightward shift of the supply curve in a market with an existing non-binding price ceiling could result in:
options: The ceiling becoming binding as equilibrium price falls below ceiling
No change as ceiling was non-binding
Increased shortage due to higher supply competing for limited demand
Ceiling remaining irrelevant as P* falls toward ceiling level
questions.explanation: Non-binding ceiling means P* > ceiling currently. If supply increases (rightward shift), P* falls. As P* approaches ceiling level, ceiling may become relevant but remains non-binding unless P* falls below ceiling. If supply increases enough that new P* < ceiling → ceiling becomes binding, creating a shortage. Hence D captures that as P* approaches ceiling, the ceiling gains relevance.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 2000 – 25P; Supply: Qs = 500 + 15P. Equilibrium P*=₹37.5. A per unit tax of ₹10 is imposed and simultaneously the government sets a price ceiling at ₹42 (above new post-tax equilibrium). Is the ceiling binding?
options: Yes, ceiling is binding — shortage occurs
No, ceiling is non-binding — market clears above the ceiling
No, ceiling is non-binding — new P* with tax falls below ₹42
questions.explanation: Tax on producers: Qs_new=500+15(P–10)=500+15P–150=350+15P. New P**: 2000–25P=350+15P → 1650=40P → P**=41.25. New P**=41.25 < ceiling=42. Ceiling is non-binding (market price 41.25 < 42). Market clears at P**=41.25. Correct: C.

questions.question: Consider two identical cities A and B. City A imposes rent control (ceiling) at ₹8000/month; City B has no control. Free market rent = ₹12000. After 10 years, which outcome is MOST LIKELY according to economic theory?
options: City A has more housing and lower rents than City B
City A has less housing, lower quality, more housing shortage than City B
Both cities converge to same rental market outcome
City B develops housing shortage due to higher market rents
questions.explanation: Long-run effects of rent control: In City A — landlords exit market, housing quality deteriorates, new construction halts, housing stock shrinks, shortage worsens. In City B — market equilibrium, housing supply responds to demand, quality maintained, no shortage. Economic theory consistently predicts City A will be worse off in long run. Correct: B.

questions.question: Demand: Qd = 4000 – 80P; Supply: Qs = 1600 + 20P. If the price ceiling is set 15% below equilibrium, what is the shortage as a multiple of equilibrium quantity?

Assertion (A): When both demand and supply are perfectly elastic, a price ceiling creates infinite shortage.
Reason (R): Perfectly elastic demand means any price below P* results in infinite demand, while perfectly elastic supply means Qs drops to zero below P*.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both true. Perfectly elastic demand (horizontal D curve at P*): any price below P* → consumers demand unlimited quantity. Perfectly elastic supply (horizontal S curve at P*): any price below P* → Qs=0. Shortage=∞–0=∞. R correctly explains the mechanism behind infinite shortage. (In practice, both being perfectly elastic is a theoretical extreme.)

questions.question: A competitive market has: P = 100 – 2Q (demand) and P = 10 + Q (supply). Calculate the total economic surplus (CS + PS) at equilibrium.
Using P = 100 – 2Q (demand) and P = 10 + Q (supply): equilibrium Q*=30, P*=40. A price floor of ₹55 is imposed. What is the DWL?
questions.explanation: Floor P=55 > P*=40 (binding). At P=55: Qd: 55=100–2Q → Q=22.5. Trade=22.5. P_supply at 22.5: P_s=10+22.5=32.5. DWL=0.5×(P_floor–P_supply_at_Qd)×(Q*–Qd)=0.5×(55–32.5)×(30–22.5)=0.5×22.5×7.5=₹84.375. Alternatively: DWL=0.5×(P*_demand at floor_Q–P*_supply at floor_Q)×(Q*–Q_floor)=0.5×(55–32.5)×7.5=₹84.375. Closest: B (₹112.5).

questions.question: NAFED (National Agriculture Cooperative Export Development Federation) buys surplus onions at MSP=₹15/kg when market price falls to ₹8/kg. Demand: Qd=50000–2000P; Supply: Qs=10000+3000P. What is NAFED's procurement bill (₹)?

Demand shifts from Qd=800–10P to Qd=1000–10P. Supply: Qs=200+20P. Old P*=20, new P*=?. If old price ceiling was at ₹18, what was the old shortage and new shortage (after demand shift, with ceiling maintained)?
questions.explanation: At the ceiling P = 18, old demand gives Qd = 800 - 180 = 620 and supply gives Qs = 200 + 360 = 560, so old shortage = 60. With new demand, Qd = 1000 - 180 = 820 while Qs remains 560. New shortage = 820 - 560 = 260.

questions.question: On a demand-supply graph, which of the following describes the area of deadweight loss from a BINDING price ceiling?
options: The triangle between D curve, S curve, and the vertical line at Qs (quantity supplied at ceiling), between ceiling price and equilibrium price
The entire rectangle between ceiling price and equilibrium price from Q=0 to Q=Qs
The area under the demand curve above the ceiling price
